# Roadmap

junto does **one thing**: a seamless, fast watch party with friends — as effortless as
pressing play on YouTube. Share a link, run one command, be watching in sync within
seconds. No accounts, no servers, no fiddling.

The concrete wedge: watching the **real file** — a 20 GB high-bitrate remux — together
in full quality. Discord screen-share tops out at 720p (1080p paid) and recompresses;
junto streams the source bytes. That sets the physics every priority call below answers
to: ~25 Mbps sustained per streaming viewer, transfers that run for an hour or more,
and 20 GB of disk per joiner. Sustained-throughput reliability outranks control-feel
polish.

Every item is judged against that one goal. **Breadth** — more players, more platforms,
more source types, live streaming, multi-room — is deliberately deferred to *Parked* at
the bottom until the core experience is flawless. When in doubt, deepen the one thing
rather than widen junto.

The two feelings that define "done": **it just works for my non-technical friend on the
first try**, and **playback starts instantly and never stalls**.

> Several items below draw on two deep-dive analyses in
> [`docs/research/`](docs/research/): a product/architecture study (transport, swarm,
> sync, GUI, distribution) and a go-to-market study. The GTM playbook lives there, not
> here — this file stays a *product* roadmap.

---

## Now — fix what's broken (July 2026 audit)

Every defect from the July 2026 full-code audit — high-severity and the
medium/low [Hardening backlog](#hardening-backlog-july-2026-audit) below,
across sync engine, data plane, control plane, app shell, and tests/CI/
distribution — has now shipped; see **Shipped**. With the audit fully closed
out, **Next** below is the natural place to look before picking up new
feature work.

---

## Hardening backlog (July 2026 audit)

Medium/low findings from the same audit, grouped by subsystem. None of these
broke a session on its own, but together they were the gap between "works in
the demo" and "robust in the field". Every subsystem below is now closed —
see **Shipped**.

**20 GB note:** at the target file size a transfer runs for one to two hours,
which upgrades data-plane reliability from "unlikely" to "expected once per
session". All four items originally flagged here are now fixed
(`Disconnected` treated as terminal, the ignored TURN-cred TTL, sidecar
resume without fsync ordering, and the unclamped priority fetch) — see
**Shipped**.

### Sync engine (`internal/syncer`)

All six findings from the audit fixed — see **Shipped**.

### Data plane (`internal/transfer`, `internal/streamserver`)

All findings from this section are fixed — see **Shipped**, including a note
on `saveProgress`'s sidecar-checkpoint sync ordering: a final SHA mismatch
still *warns and keeps the file* rather than deleting it, which is
intentional (see `TestFetchCorruptHashWarns`) and out of scope for this fix.

### Control plane (`internal/nostrx`, `internal/protocol`, mpv IPC)

All findings from this section are fixed — see **Shipped**.

### App shell (`cmd/junto`, `internal/app`, config, ytdlp, doctor)

All findings from this section are fixed — see **Shipped**.

### Tests & CI

All findings from this section are fixed — see **Shipped**.

---

## Next — finish the rough edges on the core

Still in service of the one thing, just lower on the list. Ordered by leverage against
the 20 GB wedge: keeping a multi-hour ~25 Mbps stream fed comes first, then disk and
multi-viewer realities, then control-feel polish.

| Feature | Why |
|---|---|
| **Resilient transport (QUIC + DERP)** | Raw WebRTC/STUN silently fails on symmetric NAT and CGNAT — the top "doesn't work behind my router" churn driver. The [research](docs/research/junto-product-improvement-research.md) points at the Tailscale model: coordinate over a lightweight encrypted **DERP** relay, fire simultaneous outbound UDP to punch a direct hole, and fall back to the relay (still encrypted) for the ~10% of networks that block punching — ~90% direct-connect success in practice. Crucially it stays **pure Go** via Tailscale's `tsnet`/DERP (BSD-3); iroh's QUIC stack is Rust and its Go FFI is deprecated, so it's not an option for our core. Large change (replaces the WebRTC data-channel stack and swaps TURN hosting for lightweight DERP), and the single biggest reliability win. The swarm (now shipped) runs on the existing WebRTC stack and degrades to host-only when a peer pair can't connect — this item is what turns those silent peer↔peer failures into working aux sources, so it directly raises swarm effectiveness too. *Audit note:* until this lands, the cheap interim wins are in the Hardening backlog — grace-timer on WebRTC `Disconnected` instead of terminal teardown, and lazy TTL-aware TURN creds — both of which currently turn transient flaps into hard failures. |
| **Swarm follow-ups** | Two deepenings of the shipped swarm remain (the random rarest-first tie-break shipped — see Shipped). *Primary-source re-election:* the host stays every joiner's primary (feeds the playhead window) even when an aux peer is measurably faster; re-electing the fastest source mid-file would keep the urgent front bytes off a slow host. This isn't small — "primary" is architecturally the connection running the window-follower loop, so it means synthesizing the host as an aux source, hysteresis against thrash, and untangling it from the host-migration path that also drives `SetHost`. *Cross-file rarest-first prefetch:* have aux sources fetch the next playlist file's rare pieces before the boundary — gated on **Seamless playlist transitions** below, since the downloader still processes files strictly in order (no overlap to prefetch into yet). |
| **Ephemeral read-ahead mode** | A never-write-full-copy streaming mode that keeps only a window of bytes around the playhead on disk, so watching a large file doesn't require space for the whole thing. Follow-on to the bandwidth-adaptive download window already shipped (and now that its frozen-playhead bug is fixed, actually tracks playback). Promoted from the bottom of this list: at 20 GB per movie night, "every joiner stores the whole file" is an adoption ceiling, not a nicety. |
| **Rebuffer telemetry** | Telemetry records time-to-first-frame but nothing after it, so "is streaming actually smooth in the field?" is unanswerable. Count `paused-for-cache` stalls and total stalled seconds per session (the engine already observes the property) — the one metric every smoothness item above should be judged by. (Pair it with the **Telemetry backend** item under *Under the hood* — nothing receives pings yet, so alone this measures into the void.) |
| **`/rejoin` command** | After a relay blip, the user must Ctrl-C and re-run the full join command to reconnect. A `/rejoin` that drops and re-establishes the nostr transport (keeping mpv running at the current position) closes that gap entirely — especially valuable in long sessions where re-entering the room code is annoying. *Audit note:* receive-side subscription drops are currently invisible (see Hardening backlog, control plane) — that detection work is the natural first half of this item. |
| **Faster first frame: trickle ICE over nostr** | Signaling is deliberately one SDP blob per side, which means blocking on *complete* ICE gathering (`awaitGathering`, with a timeout precisely because gathering can stall) before the first byte moves. Trickling candidates as extra relay messages cuts several seconds off time-to-first-frame — the moment a working pair connects, data flows. More signaling messages, but they're small and the transport already exists. |
| **Seamless playlist transitions** | Files download strictly in order, so file N+1 has zero bytes on disk until file N fully completes — and with the playhead-following window, N tends to finish only as its playback ends. Every playlist boundary is therefore a guaranteed re-buffer for streaming joiners. Once file N's remaining gap is small, start prefetching the head + index of N+1 so the room rolls over without a stall. (Builds on the cross-file priority fix — now shipped.) |
| **Seek-storm smoothing** | Scrubbing fires a fresh download redirect per step, each abandoning partially-fetched bytes and bouncing the room in and out of buffering. Debounce redirects to the settled position so a scrub resolves to one fetch. *Audit note:* the seek-preempt race that used to sit in this exact code path is fixed; the priority-clamp bypass (data-plane hardening backlog — a seek streams the whole remaining gap unclamped) is still worth fixing first, since debouncing on top of an unclamped fetch would still let one scrub trigger an oversized transfer. |
| **Seek-race reconciliation** | Leaderless last-writer-wins rubber-bands when two people seek at once: their state updates overwrite each other in a feedback loop. Syncplay's `ignoringOnTheFly` pattern fixes this deterministically — a seek carries a sequence integer that acts as a distributed lock, and a client ignores incoming position updates until that exact sequence is acknowledged round the room. Distinct from *seek-storm smoothing* (which debounces one user's scrub); this prevents *multiple* users' seeks from cascading. *Audit note:* build this on top of the shipped monotonic-version ratchet — sequence locks over raw wall-clock versions would inherit the clock-skew freeze-out bug. |
| **Sync over the data channel** | Pause/play/seek propagation is floored by public-relay RTT (~1 s feel); when a direct WebRTC connection already exists between peers, sync actions could ride it and fall back to the relays otherwise. The last remaining architectural speed ceiling after the persistent-connection fix. Interacts with the resilient-transport item — design them together. |
| **Playlist-shape reconciliation** | If peers have different playlist lengths, the shorter one is silently stranded at its end and stops following the room entirely. Reconcile playlists at join (extend the file-match check to shape and order) and degrade gracefully when an item is missing instead of abandoning all sync. |

---

## Under the hood

Not user-facing features, but they keep the core honest.

| Area | Description |
|---|---|
| **In-house nostr client** | `go-nostr` is archived (read-only since Jan 2026); junto is pinned at its final `v0.52.3`. No urgency (the wire protocol is frozen), but the upstream is dead. junto uses only a sliver — websocket `REQ`/`EVENT`/`CLOSE`, BIP-340 signing, NIP-44 used *symmetrically* (`sha256(room-secret)`, no ECDH) — so a hand-rolled client (a few hundred lines, keeping only `decred/secp256k1` + `x/crypto`) drops the dead dependency and its JSON-perf tree. Stability/ownership, **not** speed. Verify against NIP-44 vectors + a live relay. |
| **Sign & notarize macOS artifacts** | Developer ID-sign and notarize the binaries and the `.pkg` (and any future `.app`/`.dmg`) so Gatekeeper stops warning that junto is from an "unidentified developer". Needs a paid Apple Developer account; `quill` or `gon` in the goreleaser workflow can sign + notarize the Darwin artifacts from a **Linux** runner (`.p12` + App Store Connect `.p8` as base64 CI secrets), avoiding a slow/expensive macOS CI leg. **Priority note:** the [launch analysis](docs/research/junto-foss-launch-strategy.md) rates this *deferrable* for a CLI-only launch — early-adopter CLI users routinely bypass Gatekeeper (`xattr -cr`) — but it becomes a hard prerequisite the moment the native GUI app ships to a non-technical audience. |
| **Signed auto-update** | `junto update` already self-updates, but a P2P binary is a prime supply-chain target, so the upgrade path should verify a signature, not just TLS transport. Sign each release offline with **minisign** (Ed25519), hardcode the public key in the binary, and verify-then-atomic-swap on update (`go-selfupdate` supports this). Cheap, high-trust. Keep the signing key backed up — losing it forfeits the ability to push updates to existing installs. |
| **Telemetry backend** | The client side shipped and fires correctly, but nothing has ever been deployed to receive it — no DNS record for `telemetry.junto.watch`, no server, no way to view what would land. Right now every ping silently fails client-side (by design), so telemetry is a no-op in practice. Full setup checklist (DNS, backend options ranked by effort, the wire contract, privacy constraints to preserve): [docs/telemetry-setup.md](docs/telemetry-setup.md). |

---

## Parked — deliberately out of scope (for now)

Real ideas, but each **widens** junto instead of deepening the one thing. Revisit only
once the core watch party is flawless — and only if it still earns its keep then.

- **Live Stream URL rooms** — relay a live ffmpeg feed P2P. A different product (live, not watch-a-file-together) needing a whole parallel "live" code path. Full design preserved in [docs/stream-url-rooms.md](docs/stream-url-rooms.md).
- **Torrent / HTTP URL rooms** — alternative media sources where junto only coordinates sync (`magnet:` / direct-download URL). Breadth, not depth.
- **`/add` mid-session playlist** — append media to a running room. Nice-to-have; makes the streaming pipeline mutable at runtime for a marginal gain.
- **Room persistence + resume position** — regulars rejoin without re-sharing a code and land where the group left off ("resume at 1:12:30?"). Genuine convenience for recurring groups, but it's session bookkeeping — it deepens nothing about "starts instantly, never stalls". Moved out of *Next* (July 2026) to keep that list pure streaming reliability.
- **Multi-room** — host several parties from one process. Not the single-party experience.
- **Shell hooks** — user-defined `on_join`/`on_play`/… commands (Discord pings, smart-home). Power-user breadth.
- **More players (VLC, IINA)** — the player is mpv and you watch *in* it; supporting other players is reach, not the core feel. (The player-backend interface above still has standalone testing value. A full GUI app is its own entry below.)
- **Native GUI app (GUI watch party)** — a real double-click app with the video *built in* for the terminal-free audience, like Syncplay but with the player embedded: a non-technical friend hosts, joins, chats, and controls playback in one window, no terminal. The Go core is reusable either way — the sync engine (`internal/syncer`, fakeable via the `Player` interface), `internal/nostrx`, and `internal/transfer` are frontend-agnostic, so the app runs the existing engine as a bundled daemon and bridges to the UI rather than reimplementing sync. Two embed paths, with a real tradeoff (full analysis: [research](docs/research/junto-product-improvement-research.md)): **(a) native Swift/AppKit + libmpv** (the [IINA](https://iina.io) model) — best-in-class macOS feel, but macOS-only and a second language; **(b) Flutter + `media_kit`** — a production-ready, hardware-accelerated libmpv wrapper that is *cross-platform* (macOS/Linux/Windows in one codebase) and sidesteps the CGO/LGPL pain of binding libmpv into a Go GUI directly — the research's recommended highest-ROI path. (Note: a Wails/webview shell is **not** viable for embedded video — browser media elements lack native MKV/HEVC, defeating junto's local-file edge; webview only fits the no-embedded-video *controller* fallback.) A frictionless join then becomes possible via a `junto://<room>` OS protocol handler so the junto.watch link launches the app directly. Either path is a deliberate, large scope expansion (embedded-render layer, signed/notarized installers — see *Sign & notarize*, ongoing GUI maintenance); build it only on a stable, tagged CLI core, as a separate `cmd/`/repo so it never muddies the core.
- **Windows support** — a real reach gap (needs mpv named-pipe IPC via `go-winio`), but revisit after the core is perfect on macOS/Linux.
- **NIP-42 relay auth / NIP-17 private messaging** — protocol niceties; not felt by the user during a watch party.
- **Mobile companion app** — a "join room and control playback" remote (no video rendering) that solves "my friend is on their phone." Requires a mobile runtime story (Swift/Kotlin or React Native), a non-mpv player integration, and a separate release pipeline — a significant scope expansion, not a deepening of the core desktop experience.

---

## Shipped

- **Random rarest-first tie-break** (`internal/transfer/swarm.go`): the swarm's aux sources fetch the rarest advertised piece beyond the playhead window; ties between equally-rare pieces were broken by lowest index, so at larger scale every joiner picking the same rarity herded onto the same piece, wasting the swarm's whole point (diversity). Ties are now broken with `math/rand`'s global (concurrency-safe, auto-seeded per process, so different joiners diverge) instead of index; a package-var seam keeps it deterministic for tests. Rarest-first ordering is unchanged — only the within-rarity order randomizes. The first of the *Swarm follow-ups* in Next; the other two (primary re-election, cross-file prefetch) are larger and one is gated on Seamless playlist transitions.
- **Host upload fairness** (`internal/transfer`): when one node serves several streaming viewers at once its uplink is shared, and without coordination the OS split it evenly — the viewer with 10s of buffer was served no faster than the one with 0.5s. Since junto pauses the whole room whenever *any* viewer stalls, the room's smoothness is set by its worst-off viewer, so keeping the hungriest fed raises the stall floor for everyone. Each viewer now reports its own buffer depth (contiguous downloaded bytes ahead of its playhead) to whoever serves it, in additive `buf` data-channel frames (`Downloader.reportCushion`, sent from the run goroutine on each fetch tick and while idle-but-buffered). A serving node runs one `UploadFairness` coordinator across all its concurrent serves (`send.go`): a sender that's comfortably buffered briefly throttles itself before a chunk while another viewer *of the same file* is close to stalling, biasing the uplink toward the hungry one — capped per chunk so a satiated sender never stalls itself, scoped per file so a prefetch of a later item can't masquerade as urgent, and self-expiring so a stale report is ignored. It's a no-op with a single viewer, and a viewer that never reports a cushion (an older joiner) is neither throttled nor counted, so the path degrades exactly to the un-prioritized behavior. It stacks with the swarm rather than replacing it: the swarm spreads load across peers, but the host (and any promoted / local-file seeder) is still every joiner's primary source, so the same node runs the same fairness policy over more sources. Tested with unit coverage of the yield decision (satiated-vs-hungry, same-file scoping, staleness, single-viewer, release, bounded/cancelable pacing, nil-safety), the joiner-side cushion math, and an end-to-end check that a `buf` frame reaches the serving node's fairness table over a real WebRTC connection.
- **Interactive terminal UI** (`internal/tui`, `internal/app`, `internal/syncer`): junto's control/chat surface — everything except mpv's video — is now a redrawn TUI instead of plain scrolling text with a single pinned progress row. A gradient block-letter "JUNTO" banner, a room panel (code, join link, playlist, direct/relayed mode), a live party panel (per-peer projected position, drift, download bar, and a ready/buffering/behind/connecting badge, self pinned first, plus the pre-start "N/M ready — press Enter to start" callout), a chat/notices scrollback, and a `>` input line, all `lipgloss`-styled with adaptive light/dark colors and a rounded-panel layout that goes side-by-side when wide and stacks when narrow. Built on `charmbracelet/bubbletea`+`lipgloss`+`bubbles` (MIT, pure Go, so `CGO_ENABLED=0` cross-compiles are unaffected). It activates only when stdout is a real terminal — piped/redirected output (scripts, CI, `junto create x.mkv | cat`) stays byte-for-byte identical to before, since the session logic is one parameterized body handed either the old plain line-writer or a program-backed bridge; there is no forked copy. The bridge is a message pass (`tea.Program.Send`, safe from any goroutine) precisely because the engine's `Printf` is called concurrently from the sync loop, the sender loop, and the swarm downloader goroutines. The live panel is fed by a new nil-safe `syncer.Deps.OnSnapshot` hook off a `buildSnapshot()` that also became the single source of truth the text `/peers` output formats from (so the two can't drift). Input replaces the stdin scanner with a `bubbles/textinput` feeding the same `Lines` channel the engine already read, preserving the load-bearing bare-Enter gate; typed Ctrl-C (which raw mode delivers as a key, not SIGINT) cancels the session through the exact same `ctx.Done()` cleanup the plain path uses, and the room code + join link are reprinted to the restored terminal on exit so the alt-screen doesn't eat them. `JUNTO_NO_TUI=1` forces the plain path; `NO_COLOR` yields a monochrome full-layout TUI (the two gates are independent). Connection mode is a best-effort approximation (the relay-fallback notice flips it to "relayed"); real per-peer connection state is a documented fast-follow. Tested with `Update()`-driven model tests (line append + scrollback cap, Ctrl-C→quit, bare-Enter and typed-line submit, session-info merge, too-small-terminal, relay-mode flip, view assertions), headless `RunSession` lifecycle tests over pipes (work error propagation, parent-context cancellation), and new `syncer` tests for `buildSnapshot`/`OnSnapshot` plus a guard that the refactored `/peers` text output is unchanged. Full design: [docs/tui-design.md](docs/tui-design.md).
- **Streaming hardening (swarm)** (`internal/transfer`, `internal/protocol`, `internal/syncer`, `internal/app`): the host's upload is no longer the ceiling for the whole room — a streaming joiner now fetches byte ranges from *any* peer that has them. Built from the two pieces of prior art the research called out, on the existing WebRTC data-channel stack (peer↔peer signaling already worked over the nostr room; a pair that can't connect just falls back to host-only, never worse than before). **Per-chunk verification (BLAKE3/Bao, `lukechampine.com/blake3`)**: the host computes a bao root + outboard tree per file at create (one sequential read shared with the existing SHA-256 pass, announced as additive `FileMeta` fields); joiners fetch the small tree once over the data channel (hash-checked, persisted as a `.bao` sidecar) and verify every 256 KiB chunk group against the 32-byte root *before* it touches disk or the stream server — a corrupt or hostile source is caught at the first bad group (two strikes for the primary, permanent poisoning for an aux source) instead of the old warn-only whole-file check at the end. **Deterministic BiToS-style scheduling**: coverage rides the existing 2 s heartbeats as compact verified-piece advertisements (`Have`/`HaveDone`, hard-capped by `validate()`); the primary source (the host — `SetHost` migration unchanged) keeps feeding the playhead window exactly as before, capped to a few seconds of its own throughput per fetch once helpers are available, while up to two aux sources claim 4 MiB pieces alongside it inside the window and rarest-first beyond it. A claim table on the file entry keeps sources off each other's ranges (claims never count as "have", so nothing is mis-reported done); seeks revoke overlapping aux claims and redirect every source via per-source preempt channels; checkpoints fsync every concurrent writer before the resume sidecar snapshots (and a sidecar from a different verification regime is discarded, since its spans were never chunk-verified). **Everyone can serve**: streaming joiners answer file requests for the verified ranges they hold (`ServeFromStore` — coverage-aware: requests outside verified coverage are nak'd, never zeros from the sparse file; capped at 4 concurrent serves), local-file joiners serve and advertise their name+size-matched copies from the moment they join, and a streaming joiner whose download completes and verifies becomes host-eligible (`CanHostDynamic`), closing the last host-migration gap — a streaming-only room now survives a host drop mid-download (peers finish from each other) *and* keeps serving late joiners (a completed joiner promotes). Backward-compatible throughout: additive JSON fields and data-channel frames, no protocol version bump; old hosts get the exact pre-swarm behavior, old joiners in new rooms download exactly as before. Tested at every layer, mostly over real WebRTC loopback: bao round-trip/alignment/corruption units; a corrupt host that previously finished with a warning now fails hard (with the legacy warn-only contrast pinned); forged outboard rejection; nak-then-retry; partial-store serving (clamp/nak/stop); promotion election and data path; advertisement conversion/caps/coarsening; claim-table invariants; and three-peer swarm meshes covering two-source completion, a corrupt seeder poisoned mid-room, stale advertisements, a stalling seeder released by the watchdog, and the zero-source fallback. Follow-ups tracked in Next (*Swarm follow-ups*); the QUIC+DERP transport remains the connectivity multiplier.
- **Slow-peer recovery** (`internal/syncer`): one peer whose download couldn't keep up used to hold the *whole* room paused indefinitely — the buffering hold had no time cap, so the only escape was a human running `/kick`. This was also a flagged denial-of-service gap: a malicious peer holding its `Buffering` flag set forever could freeze everyone else with no defense (a `// SECURITY:` comment in `engine.go` tracked this pending the fix landing here). Now closed with a bounded `maxDesyncWindow` (20s): `checkDesyncTimeouts`, run every heartbeat, marks any peer whose current buffering episode has run past the window as `leftBehind`, and `anyPeerBuffering` stops honoring a `leftBehind` peer's hold — the room continues without them automatically, no human action required, regardless of whether that peer is genuinely stuck or deliberately never clearing its flag. The host also gets a manual override: pressing Enter mid-session (mirroring the existing pre-start gate's "press Enter to start anyway") immediately skips the window for whoever's currently holding the room via `overridePeerBuffering`, with the hint shown inline in the "waiting for X to buffer…" notice — host-only, since only the host is told about it. The left-behind peer isn't dropped from the room: once *their own* buffering clears, if they were stuck long enough to have been left behind, `setBuffering` now calls the existing `/sync` machinery automatically to resync to the room's live position instead of quietly resuming from wherever they paused and drifting behind for the rest of the session; an ordinary short stall (the room held the whole time, nothing to catch up on) never triggers this, avoiding noisy false-positive "resynced" messages for the common case. Each buffering episode gets its own fresh window — catching up and later falling behind again isn't held against a peer. Regression-tested end to end: the automatic timeout (both calling the check directly and through the real `heartbeat()` path), the common under-window case is unaffected, a fresh episode after catching up isn't instantly skipped, the host override works and is host-only (and is a no-op with nobody buffering), the discoverability hint shows for the host and not for other peers, and both resync-on-catchup and its absence for ordinary short stalls — ten new tests in total, each confirmed to fail against the pre-fix code before restoring the fix.
- **Disk-space preflight at join** (`internal/app`, `internal/doctor`): a streaming joiner needed the whole playlist's size free on disk — 20 GB+ for the target use case — and previously only found out via a write error deep into a multi-hour transfer. The hello already announces every file's (and subtitle sidecar's) size, so `joinStreaming` now compares the playlist total against the download directory's free space right after finding the host, and warns up front ("this room needs 21.0 GiB but /path has 8.0 GiB free — the download will likely run out of space partway through") instead of failing an hour in. It's a warning, not a hard block — matching this codebase's existing pattern for advisory join-time checks (e.g. the local-file size/name mismatch warning) — since the check can't account for a user who'll free up space mid-session, and a false block on an advisory check is worse than a warning that's occasionally too cautious. The disk-free syscall lookup (`DiskFree`, platform-specific: `statfs` on Unix, unimplemented on Windows) was already implemented for `junto doctor`'s own disk check and is now exported and reused rather than duplicated. A disk-free lookup failure (e.g. the output directory doesn't exist yet) is silently skipped — a different, pre-existing problem the download itself surfaces clearly on its own. Regression-tested: warns when the playlist exceeds free space (both numbers correctly formatted in the message), silent when there's plenty of room, silent (not a crash) when the free-space lookup itself fails, and confirmed subtitle sidecar sizes count toward the total, not just the videos — each verified to fail against the pre-fix code before restoring the fix. The full fix for tight disks remains the ephemeral read-ahead mode in Next; this is the one-evening guard rail called out for it.
- **Throughput tuning pass — measured, defaults confirmed** (`internal/transfer`): the roadmap asked whether the data channel's fixed 16 KiB chunk size and 8 MiB backpressure window (`bufferedMax`) were actually well-tuned, or just unbenchmarked defaults from before the persistent-connection rewrite — resolved by two real measured experiments instead of guesswork. `BenchmarkChunkSize` transfers a 256 MiB file over real loopback WebRTC (the best case for a chunk-size win to show up, since throughput there is CPU/per-message-overhead-bound, not network-bound) at 16/32/64/128 KiB: all four land in the same 66-69 MB/s band, and which one comes out "fastest" flips between repeated runs — the apparent few-percent gaps are run-to-run noise, not a real effect. `BenchmarkBufferedMaxUnderRTT` tests the BDP-scaling hypothesis directly, using pion's own `vnet` package (the same virtual-network mechanism pion/webrtc's test suite uses) to simulate real 20/150/300ms RTT — something no OS-level tool provides on macOS — and compares the current 8 MiB window against 32 MiB and 128 MiB at each: throughput is statistically identical across all three window sizes at every RTT tested, flatly contradicting the naive bandwidth-delay-product prediction that a bigger window should unlock far more throughput at high RTT. At 300ms RTT, measured throughput (~2.9 MB/s) lines up with pion's underlying SCTP congestion control being the actual ceiling — a layer below where `bufferedMax` operates, so raising it just queues more data waiting for SCTP to be *able* to send it rather than making SCTP send faster. Conclusion: both defaults are confirmed correct as-is; no production behavior changed. Both benchmarks are kept as permanent, reusable evidence (only run via explicit `-bench`, zero cost to normal `go test`) for anyone revisiting this tuning decision later. `chunkSize` is now a var (was a const) solely so `BenchmarkChunkSize` can vary it — production never changes it.
- **Bitrate-aware pre-buffer, stall prediction, and host upload-capacity warnings** (`internal/protocol`, `internal/transfer`, `internal/app`, `internal/syncer`): the streaming readiness cushion was a fixed 4 MB (`minPreBuffer`) — a few seconds of playback for an SD file, a blink for a 40 Mbps remux. For mp4/mov files, the host now parses its own local `moov`/`mvhd` box (handling both the 32-bit and 64-bit `mvhd` field widths, the spec's "unknown duration" sentinel, and every malformed/truncated case cleanly) to get a real duration, announces it on `protocol.FileMeta.DurationSecs` (bounded at decode like every other wire field), and the joiner sizes its pre-buffer cushion to `bitrate × 3s` (clamped 4 MB–64 MB, mirroring the existing adaptive-window clamp shape) instead of the fixed floor — which is also exactly what a non-mp4 file (or an mp4 whose duration can't be parsed) still gets, unchanged. The same bitrate now powers two proactive warnings built entirely from *real measured throughput*, not a synthetic speed test: once the pre-buffer gate opens, a joiner whose already-warmed-up download EMA looks meaningfully slower than the file's bitrate is told up front instead of finding out one stall at a time; and a new upload-side EMA on the host's `sender` (mirroring the download side's) feeds a per-file `uploadTracker` that warns once a second concurrent streaming viewer joins and the best throughput ever measured from a lone viewer can't cover both at the file's bitrate. Regression-tested throughout: the shared ISO-BMFF box-header parser extracted from the existing hardened `findMoovBox` (so its audit-fixed overflow safety is exercised by both callers, verified via the pre-existing `findMoovBox` tests passing unchanged after the refactor) plus its own fuzz target; `mvhd`/duration parsing fuzzed and unit-tested for both field widths and every malformed case; the pre-buffer clamp's floor/ceiling/formula table-tested and proven to actually gate `WaitBuffered`, not just correct in isolation; the stall warning tested via direct `Deps` injection (fires below threshold, silent above it, silent with an unknown bitrate, silent without a downloader, fires exactly once); and the upload tracker tested for the trigger, the sufficient-rate silence, the unknown-bitrate no-op, and the fire-once guard, plus a real WebRTC-loopback test forcing a known throttled rate (via a swappable reader) to prove the new upload EMA reacts to genuine slowness rather than always reporting fast-loopback numbers.
- **Closed every remaining item in the Tests & CI hardening backlog from the July 2026 audit**, closing out the section — the last one remaining: (1) `internal/mpv`'s request/response round trip (`Command`, `SetPause`, `SetSpeed`, seeks, track/subtitle changes, `GetPlaybackTime`/`GetPause`, timeouts, and mpv-side errors) now has full coverage against a fake IPC socket built the same way as the existing event-receive tests, and `requestTimeout` is now a var so the timeout test doesn't wait out the real 2s; (2) the built-in TURN credentials fetch (`InitBuiltinRelay`) now has coverage for both a successful fetch and its short startup timeout actually being shorter than the on-demand retry timeout — `builtinCredsTimeout`/`onDemandCredsTimeout` are now vars for the same reason; (3) four fuzz targets now run continuously: `findMoovBox` (remote-supplied mp4 box headers), room-code `Parse`, protocol `Decode`, and the config parser, all seeded with the existing hand-picked adversarial cases plus millions of generated ones, with zero crashes found; (4) a three-peer lossy-network harness (`lossyBroadcast`, independently dropping/duplicating/reordering delivery per recipient with a mutex-safe RNG) now runs alongside the existing two-peer lossless test, verified genuinely lossy via a 100%-loss sabotage run and stable via 15 repeated passes; (5) the three remaining transfer gaps: a hostile or buggy host's `at` frame with a negative or overflowing offset is now proven to surface as a clean `errCh` error rather than corrupt span bookkeeping (the safety net is `os.WriteAt`'s own bounds checking — no code change needed, just coverage), a corrupted or malicious `.part.junto` resume sidecar (truncated JSON, garbage bytes, a size/hash mismatch, or out-of-range spans) is now proven to fall back to a fresh download instead of panicking or resuming from bogus data, and `fetch`'s stall watchdog — a host that opens the connection, sends a few bytes, and then goes silent forever with no `eof` and no disconnect — is now covered end-to-end against a minimal but real WebRTC host double, with `connectTimeout` moved to a var and a new `stallCheckInterval` var added so the test doesn't wait out the real 30s budget; (6) `runSession` (the composition root) had its one codepath testable without a real mpv binary or live transport — a bad `MpvPath` failing in `mpv.Launch` before `t` or the `syncer.Deps` wiring is ever touched — covered, which caught a real nil-pointer panic when the error path was sabotaged; (7) CI now runs a full `goreleaser release --snapshot --clean --skip=publish` build on every push (exercising every declared OS/architecture, including linux/arm64, and catching config drift a plain `goreleaser check` wouldn't build far enough to find), the release workflow's tests now run with `-race`, and `govulncheck` is pinned to a fixed version instead of tracking `@latest`; (7b) `.goreleaser.yaml` migrated off the deprecated `brews` (Formula) config to `homebrew_casks` (see [goreleaser.com/deprecations/#brews](https://goreleaser.com/deprecations/#brews)) — `goreleaser check` now passes with zero warnings instead of hard-failing — which changes the install command to `brew install --cask swayam-mishra/tap/junto` (updated in README.md, install.sh, RELEASING.md, and `junto update`'s Homebrew message); doing so surfaced a real latent bug the migration would otherwise have shipped silently: `selfupdate.InstallMethod` only recognized Homebrew installs by a `/Cellar/` path, which a cask's `binary` stanza never uses (it symlinks from `/Caskroom/`), so a cask-installed user running `junto update`/`uninstall` would have been wrongly treated as a standalone binary instead of being told to use `brew`. Now checks for `/Caskroom/` too, regression-tested; (8) the macOS `.pkg`'s postinstall ran `brew install mpv` as root, which Homebrew refuses — silently swallowed by a trailing `|| true`, so the mpv auto-install never actually worked — postinstall now runs Homebrew as the logged-in console user, and the `.pkg` now ships a published sha256 checksum alongside it; (9) `install.sh` hardening: the whole script is now wrapped in a `main()` called only after the full function body parses, so a `curl | sh` truncated mid-download hits a syntax error instead of executing a partial prefix (verified against the old script actually running a stray `brew install`); an unauthenticated GitHub API rate limit now produces a clear explanation instead of a confusing raw error; the sudo install path now creates `/usr/local/bin` if it doesn't exist (fresh Apple Silicon machines); and every mpv package-manager branch now goes through a `run_privileged` helper that falls back to a clear manual-install message when no root/sudo is available instead of assuming sudo exists. All of it regression-verified against the pre-fix or pre-existing-gap state where a genuine bug existed (the app-composition nil-pointer panic, the sidecar span-validation bypass, the stall watchdog, the truncation-safety and rate-limit/mpv-as-root install issues), and confirmed as meaningful coverage locking in already-correct behavior where the audit's concern turned out to be a test gap rather than a live bug (the `at`-frame bounds case).
- **Fixed all nine app-shell hardening findings from the July 2026 audit** (`cmd/junto`, `internal/app`, `internal/config`, `internal/ytdlp`, `internal/doctor`, `internal/debug`, `internal/selfupdate`), closing out the section: (1) Ctrl-C during a yt-dlp fetch killed only the direct yt-dlp process, leaving any subprocess it spawned (ffmpeg, for merging) running as an orphan that kept writing to the cache and held the stdout pipe open, hanging junto on pipe drain — `download()` now runs yt-dlp in its own process group and kills the whole group on cancellation; `cleanPartials` was also unscoped, deleting *any* concurrent session's partial files from the shared cache dir, now scoped to this download's video id; (2) a subtitle like `movie.extended.en.srt` matched both `movie.mkv` and `movie.extended.mkv` when both were in the same playlist (the shorter base is a valid prefix match too), double-attaching the same file and then aborting `create` with a misleading "duplicate file name" error — subtitle discovery is now playlist-wide and assigns each file to only the video with the longest matching base; (3) `junto create <url>` ran the (potentially multi-GB) yt-dlp download before ever checking whether mpv was installed — the mpv check now runs first; (4) the config parser silently corrupted values with an inline `# comment` (kept as literal trailing text) or an escaped quote (kept the stray backslash instead of unescaping it), and a repeated key silently last-won with no indication the earlier value was discarded — all three are now hard errors, and quoted-string parsing now properly handles escapes; (5) Ctrl-C while waiting for the host printed a raw `context canceled` plus an irrelevant "get the files another way" hint, and recorded a "failure" telemetry outcome for what was actually a clean, user-initiated exit — cancellation is now detected via `errors.Is` and treated as one; (6) the yt-dlp metadata probe (`--simulate`) had no timeout, so a stalled extractor hung `create` silently with no way out short of Ctrl-C — it's now bounded to 30s; (7) the streaming pre-buffer readiness gate only closed on reaching its buffer target, so a download that failed outright (host gone, disk full, unrecoverable corruption) left that peer stuck "not ready" for the rest of an otherwise-live session — the gate now also closes on a genuine download failure, only staying open when the session itself is ending; (8) `junto doctor` collected every check's result into a slice and printed the whole checklist only once the slowest one finished, which for a relay fan-out or a full round of ICE gathering passes could mean nothing on screen for the better part of a minute — each check's result is now printed as it completes; (9) four smaller ones: the debug logger used Go's `%q` (Go-syntax quoting, not JSON) to escape string values, which produced invalid JSON for values containing certain control characters (`\a`, `\v` aren't legal JSON escapes) — now encoded with `encoding/json`; the debug log filename had only one-second resolution with no other disambiguation, so two junto processes starting in the same second silently truncated each other's log — now disambiguated by pid; `ReplaceExecutable` wrote the downloaded binary and closed it without ever syncing, so a crash between a successful rename and the OS actually flushing the write could leave the installed binary truncated or corrupt — it's now synced before the rename; and `shortURL` truncated status-line text at a fixed byte offset, which could split a multi-byte UTF-8 character in half — it now truncates on a rune boundary. All nine regression-tested (a process-group-kill test using a detached child that outlives a direct-process kill, a basename-collision test proving the longest-match rule, an ordering test proving the mpv check runs before any download starts, config-parser tests for all three malformed cases, cancellation-handling tests for both the error message and the telemetry outcome, a timeout test using a stub that never responds, readiness-gate tests covering the success/failure/session-ending cases, a real-timing test pairing an instantly-refused relay with one that hangs for the full timeout to prove results stream independently rather than being held until the slowest one finishes, and one test apiece for the four small fixes), each confirmed to fail against the pre-fix code before restoring the fix.
- **Fixed all seven control-plane hardening findings from the July 2026 audit** (`internal/nostrx`, `internal/protocol`, `internal/mpv`), closing out the section: (1) `Send` returned the instant the first relay acked, and its deferred context cancellation then aborted every still-in-flight publish to the other relays — a one-shot control message (kick/hello/signal/file-req) could durably land on just one relay, silently missed by a peer subscribed elsewhere; `Send` (via an extracted `collectAcks` helper) now waits for a second ack, up to a short grace window, before returning; (2) mpv's event channel was a fixed-size buffer with a non-blocking send, so a burst (e.g. every observed property resetting at once on a file load) silently dropped whatever didn't fit — a new coalescing `eventQueue` collapses repeated updates to the same property to the latest value instead of ever dropping one, while every other event kind (seek, playback-restart, ...) keeps its own slot; (3) `protocol.validate()` had no bound on `Signal.SDP` length, `PlayState.Position`, or `FileMeta.Size`, so a hostile or corrupted value in any of them could reach pion's SDP parser, drift-projection math, or sparse-file creation unchecked — all three are now bounded at the decode boundary; (4) the receive loop attempted a NIP-44 decrypt (an ECDH + AEAD operation) on every event matching the room's cleartext tag with no cap, inviting a decrypt-flood CPU burn from anyone who'd merely observed that tag, and a failed decrypt wasn't even counted — a fixed-window rate limiter now caps decrypt attempts per second, and both rate-limited drops and decrypt failures are now logged (throttled, not per-event); (5) relay health tracking was publish-only, so a subscription silently stalled by go-nostr's own reconnect backoff (up to 5 minutes) looked exactly like a quiet room — the receive loop now records a per-relay last-received timestamp (any event counts, including our own heartbeats echoed back, which keep arriving even in an empty room) and the session loop prints a notice when nothing's been received room-wide for 15s, recovering silently once it resumes; (6) an mpv IPC line over the old 1 MB `bufio.Scanner` limit surfaced as a fatal read error indistinguishable from mpv actually dying, tearing down the whole session over one pathological line — a new `readIPCLine` discards exactly the oversized line and resyncs at the next one instead; (7) the README's security model didn't mention that a `To`-addressed message is still decryptable by every room member, not just its recipient — addressing is routing, not privacy, now stated explicitly. All seven regression-tested (ack-timing tests against a synthetic result channel, event-coalescing and burst-survival tests including one driven through a real `net.Pipe` fake mpv socket, wire-validation boundary tests for all three new bounds, a fixed-window rate-limiter test, receive-health tests at both the transport and session-loop levels, and oversized-line resync tests at both the parser and full-client level), each confirmed to fail against the pre-fix code before restoring the fix.
- **Fixed the remaining four data-plane hardening findings from the July 2026 audit** (`internal/transfer`, `internal/streamserver`), closing out the section: (1) the `.part`→final rename and the `done` flag used to flip in two separate, unsynchronized steps, and the streamserver reopened the backing file by path on every availability run — a handler resuming right as the rename landed could read a stale "not done", try to open the now-renamed-away `.part` path, and get ENOENT, silently truncating the response right at the moment the file completed; `finishRename` now flips both under one lock, and `Store.OpenFor` opens the file once per request atomically with respect to that same lock (a POSIX fd keeps working across a rename of its underlying file, so there's no need to ever reopen); (2) `SetHost` only ever updated the pubkey a fresh `connect()` attempt would read — an attempt already in flight against the dropped host kept waiting on its offer for the full 30s `connectTimeout`, discarding the new host's offer for that whole window during the most likely migration scenario (a reconnect already underway) — `connect()` now arms a cancel func `SetHost` can call directly, so an in-flight attempt aborts within moments instead of timing out; (3) the seek-priority fetch path returned the whole remaining gap with no upper bound, so one seek near the front of a large file with nothing downloaded past it streamed the entire rest of the file in a single unbounded fetch — it's now clamped to `prioOff + windowBytes()` like the non-priority path already was; (4) four smaller ones: the mp4 box walk's bounds check (`pos+boxLen > size`) overflowed and wrapped negative on a hostile 64-bit largesize near `math.MaxInt64`, defeating the guard instead of falling back to the tail heuristic (rewritten as the overflow-safe `boxLen > size-pos`); a binary frame arriving before any `at` frame left `writePos` at its zero value, so the bytes were silently written at offset 0 and marked received instead of rejected, corrupting span bookkeeping; two playlist entries that sanitize to the same basename (distinct source paths sharing a leaf name) shared one `.part`/sidecar pair and clobbered each other's downloads, now de-duplicated with a collision-checked `-N` suffix; and `Server.Close` only called `http.Server.Shutdown`, which waits for in-flight handlers to return on their own but never cancels their request contexts, so a handler blocked in `WaitByte` on bytes that would never arrive blocked shutdown forever and leaked its goroutine — `Close` now cancels a server-wide base context (wired via `http.Server.BaseContext`) first, waking every blocked handler immediately. All four regression-tested (a deterministic stale-path test plus a concurrent-stall behavioral test for the completion race, a canceled-mid-connect test, a window-clamp unit test, an overflow-triggering largesize test, a binary-before-`at` rejection test, a basename-collision test including the pathological case where an original name already claims the suffix a duplicate would be assigned, and a blocked-handler wakeup test), each confirmed to fail against the pre-fix code before restoring the fix.
- **Fixed five data-plane hardening findings from the July 2026 audit** (`internal/transfer`): (1) a signal left queued from a dead connection attempt could be mistaken for a fresh attempt's offer, silently desyncing the offer/answer pairing until it burned the full 30s `connectTimeout` — `connect()` now discards anything queued before waiting on a new signal; (2) a host whose on-disk file was shorter than its announced `FileMeta.Size` answered a valid-offset range with an immediate `eof` and zero bytes, and `downloadFile` treated that as progress, looping forever on the never-satisfiable gap — a zero-byte completed fetch for a real gap is now a terminal error; (3) the resume sidecar was renamed into place without ever syncing the `.part` file first, so a crash right after a checkpoint could leave it claiming bytes were on disk that the OS hadn't actually flushed — `saveProgress` now syncs the file before writing the checkpoint (the final-hash-mismatch handling itself is unchanged by design: it warns and keeps the file rather than deleting it, since streaming playback may have already happened by the time the hash is checked — see `TestFetchCorruptHashWarns`); (4) built-in TURN credentials were fetched once per process behind a `sync.Once` and cached forever, so a slow network at startup could permanently disable the fallback relay for the whole session, and the server's declared TTL was ignored entirely, letting expired credentials get reused and rejected — credentials are now fetched lazily and refetched once their TTL elapses, with retry instead of permanent poisoning on failure; (5) a transient WebRTC `Disconnected` state (commonly a self-healing ICE flap) was treated exactly like `Failed`/`Closed`, tearing down and fully re-signaling over the relays — it now gets a short grace period to recover on its own first. All five regression-tested (an offer-drain integration test through `connect()`, a shorter-than-announced livelock reproduction, TURN-credential fetch/cache/retry tests against an `httptest` server, and grace-period tests using an injectable connection-state interface), each confirmed to fail against the pre-fix code before restoring the fix. Fixing the fsync-ordering issue also surfaced and fixed a related pre-existing race (moving a connection-close step relative to a checkpoint had briefly reopened a small window where an in-flight write could land after the checkpoint snapshot; resolved by separating "stop receiving" from "close the file handle").
- **Fixed six sync-engine hardening findings from the July 2026 audit** (`internal/syncer/engine.go`): (1) a kick from an already-ignored peer was still honored, letting a kicked griefer retaliate by kicking every other member — chat/state from an ignored peer were already dropped, kicks weren't; (2) `broadcast()` blocked the caller once the outbox was full for anything but a bare heartbeat state, so a sustained relay outage could stall mpv event handling, drift correction, and user input for up to 10s per queued message — it now never blocks, dropping the oldest queued message to make room; (3) a peer re-added after a >7s network blip got a brand-new zero-valued `peerInfo`, permanently losing its measured clock offset (risking a hard seek every heartbeat under real skew) and host-eligibility flag (risking a split-brain election) with no other path to re-establish either — rediscovering a peer now redoes the NTP hello exchange, and `CanHost` rides every heartbeat (not just hello) as a same-beat safety net; (4) a failed `SetSpeed`/`SetSid`/`SetAid`/`SetSubDelay`/`SeekAbsolute` still let the version commit, permanently diverging that property for the tie-break winner until a fresh action arrived — the version now only commits once every property in the round actually applied, mirroring the existing playlist-index deferral; (5) `/peers` computed drift from a peer's raw timestamp with no clock-offset correction, showing phantom ahead/behind for a peer with ordinary clock skew that the sync path itself already knows how to correct for; (6) a closed (not nil) `Buffer`/`Subs` channel is always select-ready, so it would have spun `Run`'s loop at 100% CPU forever once triggered — both are now nilled out after being observed closed. All six regression-tested (a griefer-kick guard, an outbox-saturation drop-oldest test, a rediscovery/hello-exchange test, a failed-property-defers-commit test, a clock-adjusted `/peers` test, and a closed-channel liveness test), each confirmed to fail against the pre-fix code before restoring the fix.
- **Fixed a dropped host permanently stranding a joiner** — a promoted host announced itself exactly once, and the receive side only honored that announcement from an *already-known* peer; if the one hello was lost by a best-effort relay, or the promoted peer had been evicted during the 7s outage window (so its hello arrived from a sender we'd never exchanged hellos with), the joiner's `hostPub` stayed pointed at the dead host forever — and it could never elect itself either, since it already saw the rightful new host as active and eligible. The promotion announcement now repeats for a few heartbeats (`hostAnnounceRepeats`), and is honored from an unknown sender too as long as the old host is already confirmed gone (unrestricted for a known peer, as before — that broader tradeoff is intentional, see the SECURITY comment at its call site). Regression-tested for both the unknown-sender migration path and the heartbeat repeat/cutoff behavior.
- **Fixed a captured relay event being replayable** — the receive path never checked how old an incoming event was, and go-nostr's own duplicate-event cache only catches an exact replay for about a minute. Since the room tag is cleartext, anyone who'd observed a relay could capture a signed `MsgKick`/`MsgSignal`/`MsgHello` and re-publish it verbatim after that window — re-kicking a peer or disrupting WebRTC signaling without ever knowing the room key. The receive path now rejects an event older than a 90s freshness window, more than 30s in the future, or older than the newest event already accepted from that same sender (a per-sender monotonic floor that survives for the life of the session, unlike go-nostr's short-lived cache) — closing the replay window regardless of how long after the original the attempt comes. Regression-tested with a pure, injectable freshness-check function covering all four outcomes plus the same-second-tie edge case.
- **Fixed a playlist jump hanging the whole session with no error** — files download strictly in order, and `nextFetchGap` only ever honored a seek priority for the *current* file; jumping to a later file while an earlier one was still downloading left that earlier file's playhead-following window permanently frozen (nothing reads it anymore) and reporting "nothing to fetch yet" forever, so the session never reached the file the room actually jumped to. `nextFetchGap` now rushes a file to completion (ignoring its window) as soon as a *different* file is prioritized, so `Run()` can move on; the existing in-file priority logic then takes over the instant the download reaches the prioritized file. Regression-tested end-to-end with a two-file transfer and a frozen playhead, confirmed to hang against the pre-fix code and complete in ~3s with the fix.
- **Fixed `junto update` silently installing an unverified binary** — `DownloadBinary` only checked the SHA-256 *if* the release's `checksums.txt` was present, fetchable, and listed the asset; any failure of those three conditions (missing file, a network hiccup, a name mismatch) fell through to installing the download anyway with no warning, reopening the exact tampered/corrupt-download and decompression-bomb risk the checksum check exists to close. All three failure modes are now hard errors instead of a silent skip. (Complements the minisign item under *Under the hood*, which will replace TLS-only trust with a real signature.) Regression-tested against all three unverifiable-download scenarios with an `httptest` server.
- **Fixed the pre-buffer gate leaking a premature "ready"** — `markSelfReady` correctly held a streaming joiner's readiness back until its buffer cushion was met, but the room-wide `MsgReady` announcement in `heartbeat()` fired every beat regardless of that — so the host could see the joiner as ready and start the room before the cushion existed, the exact first-frame stall the gate shipped to prevent. The announcement now only broadcasts once `selfReady` is actually true. Regression-tested by wiring an unclosed `ReadyCh` and asserting no `MsgReady` goes out until it closes.
- **Fixed telemetry never arriving, and a config typo silently dropping the opt-out** — the anonymous usage ping was posted from a goroutine spawned right before the process exits, so it was almost always killed before the request left the machine; telemetry has been dead in production. `Fire` now blocks (bounded by its existing 3 s timeout) instead of racing exit. Two config bugs compounded the privacy risk: a malformed config file was only a warning, so an unrelated typo could silently discard an explicit `telemetry = false`; and only the exact string `false` disabled it, so `off`/`0`/`False` silently left it on. A malformed config is now a hard error for the commands that read it (`create`/`join`/`doctor` — `version`/`update`/etc. are unaffected), and only `true`/`false` parse for `telemetry`. Regression-tested with an httptest server proving `Fire` blocks until delivery and the failure-reason payload carries no raw error text, plus config-parser cases for every rejected value.
- **Fixed a clock-skewed or malicious peer freezing the room's controls** — action ordering is last-writer-wins by timestamp; a peer with a fast or misconfigured clock could broadcast a timestamp far enough ahead that no real-time action from anyone else could ever outrank it, silently reverting every other peer's pauses/seeks for as long as the skew lasted — and a deliberately-sent max-value timestamp would never expire at all. `userAction` now ratchets each local action's version strictly past whatever this peer has already adopted instead of trusting the wall clock outright, and `protocol.validate` rejects incoming timestamps far outside any plausible clock skew (while still tolerating an ordinary clock that's hours or even years off). Regression-tested at both the wire-validation boundary and the engine's ratchet behavior.
- **Fixed a seek redirect race that could strand mpv on a stale in-flight range** — the downloader discarded a pending redirect signal at the *start* of each range fetch, which is after it had already decided what to fetch next; a redirect landing in that window was thrown away as a stale leftover, so mpv stayed blocked until the now-wrong in-flight range finished on its own instead of jumping to the new position immediately. The discard now happens once, before that decision is made, so a redirect is either seen fresh by the next gap decision or still pending for the fetch's own loop to catch at once. Regression-tested against a real WebRTC loopback connection.
- **Fixed the adaptive download window never tracking playback** — the local streaming server recorded the playhead once when an mpv request started (`server.go`), not as bytes were written, but mpv opens one long-lived request and reads it for minutes — so the tracked position pinned at the request's starting offset for the whole stream, and the bandwidth-adaptive window guaranteed a stall once playback outran it (once per file, and again after every seek). The server now updates the tracked position continuously as the response body is written, and no longer moves it for HEAD probes. Regression-tested by polling the tracked position mid-stream during a long-lived read.
- **Fixed mpv IPC hiccup hard-seeking the room to 0:00** — `userAction` fell back to `Position: 0` when a `GetPlaybackTime` read failed right after a pause/seek/track-switch, then broadcast that fabricated position with a fresh winning version — a single transient IPC timeout would hard-seek every other peer in the room to the start. The broadcast is now skipped on a read failure (the action already took effect locally in mpv; the next successful action re-syncs the room), and the local terminal confirmation is no longer coupled to the same read. Regression-tested with an error-injecting mpv double covering both the skip and the self-healing recovery on the next action.
- **Fixed stuck drift-correction nudge** — a rate-nudge applied to glide over small drift (`engine.go`) was only cleared by a later *winning* remote state, but the largest-pubkey peer wins every tie and so, after correcting once, never received one again — it would play up to 3% fast indefinitely, with every other peer perpetually chasing it. Heartbeats now carry a deadline (computed from the drift and the nudge's own correction rate) that force-clears an unexpired nudge as a backstop, independent of the tie-break logic. Regression-tested at both the deadline-math level and the engine's heartbeat level.
- **Fixed broken install script** — `curl junto.watch/install \| sh` 404'd for every Linux and non-Homebrew macOS user: the tarball name was built from the v-prefixed release tag (`junto_v1.2.0_…`) while goreleaser assets strip the `v` (`junto_1.2.0_…`). Fixed alongside two more landmines in the same script: a `sed` that corrupted the checksum line so the macOS `shasum -c` path always failed, and a silent skip of checksum verification (now a hard `die`) when neither `sha256sum` nor `shasum` is present. Verified end-to-end against the live v1.2.0 release.
- **Player backend interface + end-to-end loopback test** — the ~14 methods the sync engine drives on mpv are now a `Player` interface (`*mpv.Client` satisfies it), so the whole session loop is fakeable. On top of it, a two-peer in-process test runs two full `Engine.Run` loops wired together over plain channels (no relay, no real mpv — the syncer was already transport-agnostic), with a fake player that models a playback clock and emits mpv-style property/seek events. It asserts the readiness gate opens, and pause / resume / speed / seek / playlist-jump all converge, with no rebroadcast storm. It catches the class of bug (the readiness-gate property-count magic number) that previously had no regression coverage — verified by bumping the count and watching the gate assertion fail. Closes the two "Under the hood" items.
- **Graceful host migration** — when the original host drops mid-session the room no longer dies. Members advertise a `CanHost` flag in their hello; once the host is confirmed gone, the eligible local-file peer with the largest pubkey promotes itself (coordination-free — every peer computes the same winner), re-broadcasts the playlist so late joiners still find a host, and streaming joiners redirect their download to the new host on the next retry (`Downloader.SetHost`). Bytes stay hash-verified across the switch, so a redirect can never inject corrupt data. (The remaining gap — a streaming-only room with no local-file peer to promote — is now closed by the swarm entry above.)
- **Stale room detection** — if the host closes mpv or loses internet, joiners no longer hang silently. After one missed heartbeat cycle junto prints "host appears to have left — waiting…", and after a longer gap escalates to "host has been gone for X minutes — use `/quit` to exit" (both also shown in the mpv OSD). When migration promotes a replacement, the warning clears automatically.
- **Smart pre-buffer gate** — a streaming joiner no longer reports "ready" the instant mpv loads the URL (then stalls on the first frame). The readiness gate now holds until a real cushion of contiguous bytes (4 MB) sits ahead of the start of the first file, so "press play" is followed by playback, not an instant "buffering…". Local-file joiners and the host are unaffected (ready immediately).
- **File-match check** — a joiner who supplies a local copy is now warned at join time if it doesn't match the room's announced playlist: a different file count, a name that doesn't line up with the room's playlist order, or a size mismatch ("likely a different cut, expect desync"). Name and size are checked instantly; SHA-256 is left to the transfer layer's existing per-file verification, so joining stays fast. Catches a whole class of baffling silent desyncs.
- **Smooth drift correction** — small drift (0.05–0.40 s) now glides back into sync by briefly nudging mpv's playback rate (±3 %, Syncplay-style) instead of a jarring hard seek; hard seeks are reserved for larger gaps. The nudge is purely local — it never enters heartbeats or the last-writer-wins speed channel — so each peer corrects its own drift without dragging the room's speed along. Makes routine sync invisible.
- **junto.watch/join page** — the one-tap invite link now lands on a real page (served from the separate web repo): it reads the room code from the URL fragment (never sent to the server), validates it, and shows the two install steps plus the pre-filled `junto join <code>` command. The page is deliberately `noindex`, ships zero analytics, and is excluded from the sitemap so a leaked invite can't be crawled.
- **One-command install** — `curl junto.watch/install \| sh` detects OS/arch, downloads the release binary (SHA-256-verified against `checksums.txt`), installs it to `/usr/local/bin` (or `~/.local/bin`), and installs mpv via the system package manager (brew/apt/dnf/pacman/zypper); on macOS it prefers the Homebrew tap when present. A double-click macOS `.pkg` (built and uploaded by the release workflow on a macOS runner, with a `brew install mpv` postinstall) is produced on each release. The installer script lives at `scripts/install.sh`; `junto.watch/install` just needs to serve it. Removes the "install Homebrew first" ceiling on the first-try promise.
- **Guided failure & preflight** — mpv is now checked *before* the relay connect, so a missing player fails instantly with install steps instead of after a ~10 s wait. Connection/relay/NAT errors auto-suggest `junto doctor`, and the "NAT traversal failed" jargon is rewritten in plain English. `junto doctor` always ends with a one-line verdict (safe / not ready to stream) and reports the built-in relay and telemetry status.
- **Built-in TURN relay** — when direct hole-punching fails, junto fetches short-lived HMAC credentials for a built-in relay (1 s timeout, single constant for the endpoint) and retries through it automatically — zero flags, transparent symmetric-NAT recovery. Falls back to direct-only if the relay is unreachable; `-turn` still overrides. The fallback plumbing (`transfer.FallbackRelays`) is now populated at session start by `transfer.InitBuiltinRelay`.
- **Audio-track sync** — the active audio track (`aid`) is mirrored across the room exactly like the subtitle track, so multi-audio files (dubs, multi-language releases) no longer silently desync when one person switches dub. (Fixing the readiness-gate property count from a hard-coded `5` to `7` came along for free.)
- **Opt-in telemetry** — a single anonymous HTTPS ping on `create`/`join` reports event type, version, OS/arch, outcome + high-level reason, relay latency distribution, and (streaming joiners) time-to-first-frame and transfer success — no PII, no room secret, no file names. Opt out with `--no-telemetry` or `telemetry = false`; first run discloses exactly what's collected. Fire-and-forget with a 3 s hard timeout; silent on failure.
- **Structured logging (`-debug`)** — `-debug` writes timestamped JSON event lines (relay health, ICE negotiation, downloads, peer join/leave, sync decisions, drift corrections) to `~/.cache/junto/debug-<timestamp>.log`. Off by default so normal runs stay clean; pairs with telemetry — when a failure pings, `-debug` is the next step.
- **Buffer-free playback** — the room now pauses only on mpv's *real* playback starvation (`paused-for-cache`), never on speculative read-ahead, so a large jitter buffer (mpv demuxer read-ahead raised to 120 s / 128 MiB) and an instant start finally coexist — curing the "minutes cached but still frozen" stall. The background download follows the playhead in a **bandwidth-adaptive window** sized to measured throughput (32 MB–512 MB), instead of racing to fill the whole file front-to-back; the index/tail prefetch and seek redirects bypass the window so startup and seeks stay instant. (An ephemeral, never-write-full-copy mode remains a follow-on — see Next.)
- **In-player notices (mpv OSD)** — chat, joins/leaves, buffering, pause/resume/seek, kicks, relay reconnects, and the "press Enter to start" prompt now show as OSD overlays *inside* the mpv window (where people are actually looking), mirrored alongside the existing terminal output. The gate prompt stays pinned while waiting, and notices are dispatched off the sync loop so a stalled mpv IPC can never freeze the room.
- **Clock offset estimation** — peers measure their clock skew NTP-style on the hello handshake (round-trip timestamps echoed both ways) and compensate every projected position, so a machine whose clock is off no longer looks like drift. With skew removed, the hard-seek drift threshold tightened from 1.0 s to 0.5 s for sharper sync, while the seek-suppression tolerance stays looser (1.0 s) so coarse-keyframe landings aren't mistaken for user seeks. Implausible (jittery) measurements are rejected so a noisy sample can't make sync worse than none.
- **Relay health scoring** — each relay's publish latency and consecutive failures are tracked; sends go to the reachable relays fastest-first, and a relay that fails repeatedly is benched for a cooldown (with a safety-net fallback if every relay is down), so play/pause/seek land through the snappiest path. `junto doctor` now reports each relay's round-trip latency.
- **One-tap join link** — `junto create` puts `https://junto.watch/join#<code>` on the clipboard; the junto.watch `/join` page shows friends how to join (install + the `junto join <code>` command). The code rides in the URL **fragment**, so the web host never sees the room secret — relays already see only ciphertext. No more copying a 30-character code over chat.
- **yt-dlp URL sources** — `junto create <url>` (YouTube or any yt-dlp-supported site) downloads via yt-dlp and hosts the result like a local file: 1080p cap by default (`-quality`), cached in `~/.cache/junto` keyed by video id, live URLs rejected up front. No browser extension.
- **`junto update` / `junto uninstall`** — self-update a standalone binary (checksum-verified; Homebrew/`go install` builds are pointed at the right command) and clean removal (optionally with config + cache via `-purge`).
- **Persistent transfer connection** — one WebRTC connection now serves a whole file, with byte ranges negotiated on the data channel; the old model rebuilt the connection (ICE + SDP over relays) every 4 MiB, which throttled throughput below HD bitrates and caused constant streaming stalls. A seek redirects the live stream mid-flight instead of reconnecting, and the backpressure window grew from 1 MiB to 8 MiB so fast links aren't capped by round-trip latency.
- **Precise mp4 index prefetch** — for mp4/mov, junto walks the box structure to fetch the `moov` atom *exactly*, wherever and however large it is, instead of guessing a fixed 16 MiB tail (which missed the oversized `moov` of long/4K files and stalled startup). Other containers keep the tail heuristic.
- **Tuned mpv streaming read-ahead** — streaming joiners run mpv with a small read-ahead on top of the bytes already downloaded ahead of the playhead (the real jitter cushion), so playback starts fast instead of waiting to fill a huge window. Trade-off: smoothness rides on the download outrunning the bitrate — see README Limitations.
- **Config file** — `~/.config/junto/config.toml` persists nick, relays, mpv path, and TURN credentials; flags still override
- **Subtitle file sharing** — the host offers sidecar `.srt`/`.ass` files matching a video; joiners fetch them first and load them into mpv, so subtitle sync works without sourcing subs separately
- **Cross-session resumable downloads** — quitting mid-download keeps the `.part` + a span sidecar, so rejoining resumes instead of restarting
- **`/ignore`, `/kick`, `/sync`** — locally mute a peer, cooperatively kick one (everyone auto-ignores), or force a resync to the room without rejoining
- **Richer `/peers`** — each peer's position, drift, download progress, and buffering/ignored state
- **Binary releases (v1.0.0)** — macOS (universal) and Linux (amd64/arm64) binaries via goreleaser; `brew install --cask swayam-mishra/tap/junto` pulls in mpv automatically
- **`junto doctor`** — preflight checks for mpv, relay reachability, NAT traversal (STUN), the TURN relay if configured, disk space, and yt-dlp/ffmpeg, with a fix printed for whatever fails
- **One-line invite** — `junto create` puts the paste-ready join command on the clipboard
- **Flags anywhere** — `junto join <code> -out dir` works the same as `junto join -out dir <code>`
- **Seek-ahead buffering** — seeking past the downloaded bytes steers the download to fetch that region first (random-access, not just front-to-back) and pauses the whole room with a "buffering…" notice until everyone's caught up, then resumes together — no silent stall or desync
- **Relay resilience** — go-nostr silently re-dials dropped relays; a "reconnecting…" / "reconnected" notice (driven by heartbeat send-health) is now the worst a user sees during a blip, never a frozen room
- **Helpful error messages** — low-level failures (bad room code, host not running, mpv missing/closed, NAT traversal, no relays) are replaced with plain-English, actionable guidance
- **Readiness gate** — the host holds at a start screen showing who's loaded and ready ("2/3 ready"); one keypress (Enter) starts playback for the room, so nobody's left guessing whether everyone's at the beginning
- **Transfer progress bar** — a live, in-place bar during P2P download showing percent, bytes received, smoothed speed, and ETA; pinned to the bottom row so chat scrolls above it without interleaving
- **TURN relay support** — `-turn`, `-turn-user`, `-turn-pass` flags for when direct hole-punching fails
- **Streaming playback** — files play immediately via a local HTTP range server; the whole playlist streams while downloading in the background
- **Tail-prefetch** — the last 16 MiB of each file is fetched first so files with end-of-file indexes (non-faststart mp4, end-Cues mkv) start quickly
- **Resumable transfers** — a dropped connection resumes from the last received byte; the running hash is rebuilt from data already on disk
- **Subtitle sync** — active subtitle track and delay offset are mirrored across the room via mpv IPC
- **Playback speed sync** — playback rate is synced; drift projection accounts for non-1× speeds
- **Playlist support** — host queues multiple files; everyone advances together at EOF or on an explicit skip; joiners fetch every entry P2P
- **Nick colors** — each peer gets a consistent terminal color derived from their public key; respects `NO_COLOR`
- **Late-joiner position** — new peers receive a full state snapshot on connect and land at the room's current item, position, speed, and subtitle state in one relay round-trip
